title: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium
authors: Rodney H. Falk, Neal K. Lakdawala, Lynne Warner Stevenson, Joseph Loscalzo

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Key Features of Restrictive Cardiomyopathy (RCM)
items.content: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is characterized by abnormal diastolic function due to noncompliant ventricles, with mildly decreased contractility and ejection fraction. Both atria are enlarged, and end-diastolic pressures are elevated in both ventricles.
keyPoints: Ejection fraction usually 30–50%
Both atria are enlarged
End-diastolic pressures are elevated in both ventricles
Subtle exercise intolerance is often the first symptom
Right-sided symptoms like edema and ascites may be more prominent
A fourth heart sound is common, while a third heart sound is less so
Jugular venous pressures show rapid Y descents; positive Kussmaul sign possible
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.title: Management Strategies for RCM
items.content: In the absence of specific therapies, management focuses on maintaining optimal volume status. Beta blockers can reduce heart rate and contractility but may decrease functional reserve. Vasodilation is often poorly tolerated.
keyPoints: Optimal volume status involves balancing ventricular filling and systemic decongestion
Beta blockers are used to manage symptoms by reducing heart rate and contractility
Vasodilation is generally not well-tolerated
Therapies effective for dilated cardiomyopathy are not recommended for RCM
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment Specificity in Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: In cardiac amyloidosis, determining the specific precursor protein is crucial for appropriate treatment. The two main types are transthyretin (TTR) amyloidosis, which can be due to a genetic variant (ATTRv) or wild-type TTR (ATTRwt), and light chain amyloidosis from abnormal plasma cells.
keyPoints: Amyloid deposits stain with Congo red and show apple-green birefringence under polarized light microscopy.
Electron microscopy reveals nonbranching fibrils ~10 nm in diameter.
TTR amyloidosis can be ATTRv or ATTRwt, with variant TTR generally less stable than wild-type.
Treatment is specific to the type of amyloid; incorrect typing leads to inappropriate therapy.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.title: Pathophysiology and Diagnostic Features of Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: Cardiac amyloidosis leads to increased heart mass due to extracellular amyloid deposits, causing thickened ventricular walls with decreased compliance. This results in a restrictive pathophysiology with small left ventricular cavities and often normal-sized right ventricles that may dilate in advanced stages.
keyPoints: Extracellular amyloid increases heart mass by expanding the extracellular space.
Both left and right ventricular walls thicken, leading to decreased compliance.
Left ventricular cavity is relatively small; right ventricle size is normal but may dilate in advanced disease.
Atrial dilation occurs due to chronic elevation of biventricular diastolic filling pressure.
Endomyocardial biopsy is almost always diagnostic for cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.title: Recognizing Cardiac Amyloidosis through Right Heart Failure and Arrhythmias
items.content: Cardiac amyloidosis often presents with symptoms of right heart failure, such as peripheral edema and ascites. Despite this clinical predominance, cardiac catheterization reveals elevated pressures in both ventricles. Atrial fibrillation is a common arrhythmia, especially in TTR amyloidosis, potentially leading to severe dyspnea.
keyPoints: Common presentation: congestive heart failure with predominant right heart failure symptoms (peripheral edema, ascites).
Cardiac catheterization reveals elevated left and right ventricular filling pressures despite clinical predominance of right ventricular failure.
Atrial fibrillation is a common presenting feature in TTR amyloidosis, potentially causing severe dyspnea.
Refractory atrial arrhythmia raises suspicion for TTR cardiac amyloidosis, particularly in patients over mid-60s without other causes.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.title: Physical Examination and Systemic Signs in Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: In cardiac amyloidosis, physical examination reveals normal or low pulse pressure, elevated jugular venous pressure with possible Kussmaul sign. Heart sounds are typically normal, and murmurs are rare except for occasional tricuspid regurgitation or aortic stenosis in TTR amyloidosis. Systemic signs include hepatomegaly, macroglossia, and periorbital bruising suggesting AL amyloidosis.
keyPoints: Physical exam: normal/low pulse pressure, elevated jugular venous pressure with possible Kussmaul sign.
Heart sounds are usually normal; third and fourth heart sounds are rare due to restrictive pathophysiology.
Pleural effusions common; murmurs of tricuspid regurgitation or aortic stenosis may be present (aortic stenosis associated with TTR amyloidosis).
Systemic signs: hepatomegaly, macroglossia, periorbital bruising suggest AL amyloidosis; carpal tunnel syndrome or ruptured biceps tendon suggest TTR amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.title: Distinguishing AL Amyloidosis from Hypertensive Heart Disease and Other Cardiomyopathies
items.content: AL amyloidosis can present with biventricular failure, edema, and a thick left ventricle without severe hypertension history. Unlike hypertensive heart disease, which shows ECG evidence of LVH after years of uncontrolled hypertension, AL amyloidosis rarely does. Other conditions like Fabry disease show typical skin manifestations and usually have LVH on the ECG. Mitochondrial cardiomyopathies may mimic echocardiographic features but differ in noncardiac symptoms.
keyPoints: AL amyloidosis can present with biventricular failure, edema, and thick left ventricle without severe hypertension history.
Hypertensive heart disease usually shows LVH on ECG after years of uncontrolled hypertension; uncommon in amyloidosis.
Fabry disease often presents with skin manifestations and LVH on the ECG.
Mitochondrial cardiomyopathies may have similar echocardiographic features but differ in noncardiac symptoms like diabetes, deafness, and strokes.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.title: Identifying Non-Cardiac Clues of AL Amyloidosis
items.content: AL amyloidosis is a multisystem disease often presenting with heart and kidney involvement. Key noncardiac clues include significant proteinuria, neuropathy, periorbital purpura (20–30% of cases), and macroglossia (~10%). These features help distinguish AL amyloidosis from other causes of congestive heart failure.
keyPoints: AL amyloidosis is a multisystem disease with common involvement of the heart and kidneys.
Significant proteinuria, neuropathy, periorbital purpura (20–30%), and macroglossia (~10%) are noncardiac clues.
Periorbital purpura is virtually pathognomonic of AL amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.title: Identifying Variant TTR Amyloidosis and Its Presentation
items.content: Variant TTR amyloidosis, particularly the Val122Ile variant, presents distinctively with possible mild sensory neuropathy or severe sensorimotor neuropathy. Cardiac symptoms may be masked by physical activity limitations due to neuropathy or reduced peripheral resistance from autonomic involvement.
keyPoints: Val122Ile is common in ~3.5% of the U.S. African descent population, presenting as late-onset restrictive cardiomyopathy usually in the seventh decade onward.
Neuropathy can be mild or absent but carpal tunnel syndrome often precedes heart failure by several years.
Median survival post-heart failure onset is 2–3 years without treatment.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.title: Understanding Wild-Type TTR Amyloidosis Presentation and Demographics
items.content: Wild-type TTR amyloidosis primarily affects older men, with a median survival of 4–5 years from heart failure onset. It is characterized by progressive congestive heart failure rather than sudden death.
keyPoints: Most common form of cardiac amyloidosis, affecting mainly men in their seventh to ninth decades.
Men are almost 20 times more likely to be diagnosed compared to women.
Untreated median survival from heart failure onset is around 4–5 years.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.title: Recognizing Subclinical and Clinical Features of TTR Cardiomyopathy
items.content: Both wild-type and mutant TTR cardiomyopathies have a subclinical phase with progressive amyloid infiltration. Atrial arrhythmias may present as the initial symptom, often precipitating or worsening heart failure.
keyPoints: Subclinical phase involves several years of progressive cardiac amyloid infiltration.
30–50% of involved heart weight is due to amyloid by heart failure onset.
Atrial fibrillation or flutter can be presenting features and may worsen heart failure.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.title: Echocardiographic Features Suggestive of Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: Echocardiography is crucial in diagnosing cardiac amyloidosis, revealing increased left ventricular wall thickness without dilation and frequent biatrial enlargement. A distinctive 'bull’s-eye' pattern on longitudinal strain imaging indicates preserved apex strain with impaired base strain.
keyPoints: Increased left ventricular wall thickness without dilation
Biatrial enlargement often present
'Bull’s-eye' appearance in longitudinal strain imaging highly suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Role of Cardiac Magnetic Resonance and Nuclear Imaging in Amyloidosis Diagnosis
items.content: Cardiac magnetic resonance shows abnormal ventricular thickening, expanded extracellular volume, and delayed gadolinium enhancement. Technetium tracers (pyrophosphate or DPD) help differentiate amyloid types; uptake suggests transthyretin amyloidosis if other tests are negative.
keyPoints: Abnormal ventricular thickening and marked expansion of the extracellular space on cardiac magnetic resonance
Extensive delayed gadolinium enhancement
Technetium tracers not usually taken up by normal heart; uptake suggests transthyretin amyloidosis if other tests are negative
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Confirmatory Testing for Light Chain Amyloidosis
items.content: In suspected light chain amyloidosis, elevated serum free light chains necessitate tissue biopsy confirmation. Subcutaneous fat pad or skin biopsies often reveal amyloid deposits, avoiding cardiac biopsy. If negative, endomyocardial biopsy is recommended.
keyPoints: Elevation of serum free lambda or kappa light chains indicates plasma cell dyscrasia
Subcutaneous fat pad or skin biopsies show amyloid in most cases
Endomyocardial biopsy positive in over 90% if amyloid heart disease is present
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.title: Why NTproBNP and Troponin Levels are Key in Suspected Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: In patients with suspected cardiac amyloidosis, NTproBNP levels may be disproportionately high relative to the degree of congestive heart failure, often ranging from 1000 to 2000 pg/mL even with mild symptoms. Additionally, troponin is frequently mildly elevated, which can lead to a misdiagnosis of ischemic heart disease.
keyPoints: NTproBNP levels may be in the range of 1000 to 2000 pg/mL despite mild congestive heart failure.
High-sensitivity troponin is often mildly elevated and can mimic ischemic heart disease.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.title: How to Diagnose AL Amyloidosis: The Role of Immunofixation and Biopsy
items.content: In suspected AL amyloidosis, serum and urine immunofixation are more sensitive than protein electrophoresis for detecting monoclonal gammopathy. A tissue biopsy is essential for diagnosis as there is no definitive noninvasive test; subcutaneous fat pad aspirate or deep-skin needle aspiration can be positive if evaluated by a skilled pathologist.
keyPoints: Serum and urine immunofixation are more sensitive than protein electrophoresis in detecting monoclonal gammopathy.
A tissue biopsy is necessary for diagnosing AL amyloidosis as there is no definitive noninvasive test.
Subcutaneous fat pad aspirate or deep-skin needle aspiration biopsy can be positive if evaluated by a skilled pathologist.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.title: Management of Heart Failure in Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: In cardiac amyloidosis, sodium restriction is crucial for managing heart failure due to the restrictive pathophysiology that makes patients sensitive to blood volume changes. Diuretics are central to treatment, with a combination of loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide or torsemide) and spironolactone or eplerenone being well-tolerated.
keyPoints: Sodium restriction improves response to diuretics due to restrictive pathophysiology.
Diuretics are the mainstay of therapy; loop diuretics combined with spironolactone/eplerenone is recommended.
Monitor renal function when adjusting diuretic doses, especially in older patients with ATTRw amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.title: Treatment of Cardiac Arrhythmias and Anticoagulation
items.content: Atrial fibrillation or flutter is common in cardiac amyloidosis, often leading to clinical deterioration. Anticoagulation is mandatory due to high thromboembolism risk, with no increased bleeding risk noted. Amiodarone is the preferred antiarrhythmic for atrial arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Atrial fibrillation/flutter common; associated with clinical deterioration.
Anticoagulation is mandatory in patients with atrial fibrillation/flutter due to high thromboembolism risk.
Amiodarone is the most effective antiarrhythmic for atrial arrhythmias.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.title: Pacing Considerations in TTR Amyloidosis
items.content: Patients with TTR amyloidosis are at high risk for developing high-degree atrioventricular block, often preceded by bifascicular or left bundle branch block. Biventricular pacing is recommended over right ventricular pacing to avoid septal contraction abnormalities that can decrease cardiac output.
keyPoints: High risk of high-degree AV block in TTR amyloidosis; preceded by ECG changes like bifascicular block.
Consider cardiac pacing for unexplained dizziness or syncope with these ECG abnormalities.
Biventricular pacing is preferred to avoid septal contraction abnormalities and maintain cardiac output.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS
items.title: Management of Cardiac Involvement in AL Amyloidosis
items.content: The treatment for cardiac involvement in AL amyloidosis involves a combination therapy with daratumumab, bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, and dexamethasone. Close monitoring by cardiologists is essential due to potential complications such as sodium retention from dexamethasone, which may necessitate increased diuretic dosing or reduced steroid use. Bortezomib can cause reversible cardiac toxicity and exacerbate neuropathy. High-dose melphalan with autologous stem cell transplantation requires thorough pretherapy cardiac evaluation due to risks like fluid retention and arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Common regimen: daratumumab, bortezomib, cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone
Dexamethasone may worsen sodium retention; adjust diuretics or reduce dose as needed
Bortezomib can cause reversible cardiac toxicity and neuropathy exacerbation
High-dose melphalan with stem cell transplantation requires pretherapy cardiac evaluation
Post-chemotherapy improvement in heart failure is possible but not guaranteed
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.title: Treatment Strategies for TTR Cardiac Amyloidosis
items.content: The treatment goal for TTR cardiac amyloidosis is to reduce or halt further amyloid deposition. Tafamidis, which stabilizes transthyretin tetramers, slows disease progression and may halt it if administered early. Acoramidis shows similar efficacy. TTR silencers, reducing hepatic transthyretin production, are approved for familial amyloid polyneuropathy in the U.S., with eplontersen and vutrisiran undergoing trials for cardiomyopathy.
keyPoints: Tafamidis stabilizes transthyretin tetramers to slow progression
Acoramidis shows similar efficacy as tafamidis
TTR silencers reduce hepatic production of transthyretin
Eplontersen and vutrisiran are in trials for TTR amyloid cardiomyopathy
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is a common characteristic of restrictive cardiomyopathy (RCM)?
options: Elevated end-diastolic pressures in both ventricles with preservation of resting cardiac output until late in the disease.
Significant left ventricular dilation and decreased ejection fraction below 30%.
Dynamic displacement of the cardiac impulse similar to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
Common presence of a third heart sound in sinus rhythm.
items.explanation: Elevated end-diastolic pressures are typical, with preserved cardiac output until late stages.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended routine therapy for restrictive cardiomyopathy (RCM)?
options: Beta blockers to reduce heart rate and contractility.
Therapies proven effective for dilated cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction.
Maintaining optimal volume status as a compromise between adequate ventricular filling and systemic decongestion.
items.explanation: Therapies for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction are not recommended for RCM.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.question: Which of the following is a common symptom presentation in restrictive cardiomyopathy (RCM)?
options: Dominant right-sided heart failure symptoms such as edema, abdominal discomfort, and ascites.
Subtle exercise intolerance often recognized before congestive symptoms.
Significant left ventricular enlargement with dynamic cardiac impulse.
Common presence of atrial fibrillation without elevated jugular venous pressures.
items.explanation: RCM often presents with right-sided heart failure symptoms due to elevated filling pressures in both ventricles.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: Which staining characteristic is used to identify amyloid deposits in cardiac tissue?
options: Stains with Congo red and exhibits apple-green birefringence under polarized light
Stains blue with hematoxylin and eosin
Does not stain but appears as clear deposits
items.explanation: Amyloid is identified by its staining characteristics: it stains with Congo red and exhibits apple-green birefringence under polarized light.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: What is the typical diameter of amyloid fibrils as seen under electron microscopy?
items.explanation: Amyloid deposits consist of nonbranching fibrils ~10 nm in diameter as seen under electron microscopy.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: Which precursor protein is generally considered less stable, leading to variant TTR amyloidosis?
options: Light chains produced by abnormal plasma cells
None; all forms are equally stable
items.explanation: Variant TTR is generally considered to be less stable than wild-type TTR, leading to ATTRv amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.question: Which clinical feature is more commonly associated with TTR amyloidosis compared to AL amyloidosis?
options: Atrial fibrillation as a presenting feature
Refractory atrial arrhythmia unresponsive to ablation
items.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is more commonly associated with TTR amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.question: What physical examination finding suggests the presence of cardiac amyloidosis?
options: Elevated jugular venous pressure with Kussmaul sign
items.explanation: Cardiac amyloidosis often presents with elevated jugular venous pressure and a paradoxical rise on inspiration (Kussmaul sign).
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.question: Which condition is associated with an unusually hard liver in cardiac amyloidosis?
items.explanation: An unusually hard liver due to amyloid infiltration is associated with AL amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.question: Which condition is less likely to present with peripheral edema and usually shows left ventricular hypertrophy on ECG?
items.explanation: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy rarely presents with peripheral edema and usually has left ventricular hypertrophy on the ECG.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.question: Which of the following conditions can be distinguished from amyloidosis by typical skin manifestations?
items.explanation: Fabry disease may have typical skin manifestations and usually left ventricular hypertrophy on the ECG.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.question: Which condition was a major differential diagnosis of amyloid heart disease before the advent of echocardiography?
items.explanation: Before echocardiography, constrictive pericarditis was a major differential diagnosis of amyloid heart disease.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.question: Which TTR variant is most common in causing amyloid cardiomyopathy among African Americans in the United States?
items.explanation: The Val122Ile variant is the most common TTR variant causing amyloid cardiomyopathy in African Americans in the U.S.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.question: What is a common early symptom that may precede heart failure by several years in patients with Val122Ile variant TTR amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Carpal tunnel syndrome often precedes the onset of heart failure by several years in patients with the Val122Ile variant.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.question: What is a typical presenting feature of wild-type TTR amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Atrial arrhythmias, most commonly atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter, may be the presenting feature of wild-type TTR amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.question: In untreated patients with Val122Ile variant TTR amyloid cardiomyopathy, what is the median survival following the onset of heart failure?
items.explanation: The median survival for untreated patients with Val122Ile variant TTR amyloid cardiomyopathy is 2–3 years following the onset of heart failure.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.question: Which imaging modality can show increased left ventricular wall thickness without ventricular dilation in cardiac amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is noted for showing increased left ventricular wall thickness without dilation in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What pattern on longitudinal strain imaging is highly suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis?
options: Uniformly impaired strain throughout the heart
Preserved strain at the apex and impaired strain at the base
Impaired strain at the apex and preserved strain at the base
items.explanation: A 'bull’s-eye' appearance with preserved strain at the apex and severely impaired strain at the base is suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which tracer uptake pattern helps differentiate transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy from other types?
options: Avoidance of tracer uptake in patients with transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy
Uniform tracer uptake across all forms of cardiac pathology
items.explanation: Tracers like technetium pyrophosphate are not usually taken up by the normal heart or other cardiac pathologies but may be in transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is a critical step if light chain amyloidosis remains a possibility despite a positive pyrophosphate scan?
items.explanation: Consulting with a hematologist is important to help clarify the diagnosis if light chain amyloidosis remains possible.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.question: Which laboratory finding in AL amyloidosis may lead to a misdiagnosis of ischemic heart disease?
items.explanation: High-sensitivity troponin is frequently mildly elevated in AL amyloidosis, potentially leading to a misdiagnosis of ischemic heart disease.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.question: In patients with suspected AL amyloidosis, which test is more sensitive than serum or urine protein electrophoresis for detecting monoclonal gammopathy?
items.explanation: Serum and urine immunofixation are more sensitive than serum or urine protein electrophoresis in detecting monoclonal gammopathy in AL amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.question: Which test is recommended for typing the amyloid to avoid diagnostic uncertainty?
items.explanation: Mass spectrometry is recommended for typing amyloid to avoid diagnostic uncertainty.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.question: Which combination of diuretics is generally well-tolerated in managing congestive heart failure in patients with cardiac amyloidosis?
items.explanation: The combination of a loop diuretic such as furosemide or torsemide with spironolactone or eplerenone is usually well tolerated.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.question: Why are angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors generally avoided in patients with light chain amyloidosis?
options: They increase the risk of thromboembolism.
They may cause profound hypotension due to impaired autonomic function.
They lead to increased sodium retention.
They are associated with a high incidence of pulmonary embolism.
items.explanation: In light chain amyloidosis, autonomic function is often impaired, and the use of angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors may be associated with profound hypotension.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.question: What is a common cardiac arrhythmia in both AL and TTR amyloidosis that is frequently associated with clinical deterioration?
items.explanation: The most frequently seen arrhythmias in both AL and TTR amyloidosis are atrial fibrillation or flutter, often associated with clinical deterioration.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.question: What is the recommended approach for managing atrial fibrillation or flutter in patients with cardiac amyloidosis?
options: Use of beta-blockers as first-line therapy
Anticoagulation and consideration of restoring sinus rhythm
items.explanation: Anticoagulation is mandatory in atrial fibrillation or flutter, and restoration of sinus rhythm should be considered since rate-controlling agents are poorly tolerated.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: Which medication used in the treatment of light chain amyloidosis may cause acute, reversible cardiac toxicity?
items.explanation: Bortezomib may rarely cause acute, reversible cardiac toxicity.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: What is the primary goal of treatment for TTR cardiac amyloidosis?
options: To reduce or stop further amyloid deposition
items.explanation: The aim is to reduce or stop any further amyloid deposition.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.question: Which drug stabilizes the tetrameric structure of transthyretin and is used in treating TTR cardiac amyloidosis?
items.explanation: Tafamidis stabilizes the tetrameric structure of transthyretin.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: Restrictive cardiomyopathy is primarily characterized by significantly reduced ejection fraction.
items.explanation: RCM typically has a mildly decreased contractility and ejection fraction (usually 30–50%).
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY
items.statement: A fourth heart sound is more common than a third heart sound in sinus rhythm for patients with restrictive cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: In RCM, a fourth heart sound is indeed more common than a third heart sound.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies | RESTRICTIVE CARDIOMYOPATHY

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.statement: Endomyocardial biopsy is almost always diagnostic in cases of cardiac amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Histologically, amyloid deposits are widespread throughout the heart so that endomyocardial biopsy is almost always diagnostic.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.statement: The left ventricular cavity in cardiac amyloidosis is typically enlarged.
items.explanation: In cardiac amyloidosis, the left ventricular cavity may be relatively small due to increased wall thickness and decreased compliance.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.statement: A third heart sound is commonly heard in patients with cardiac amyloidosis.
items.explanation: A third heart sound is very rarely heard due to the restrictive pathophysiology of cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.explanation: Macroglossia is a feature that strongly suggests AL amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · CLINICAL FEATURES OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.statement: AL amyloidosis is closely related to multiple myeloma and may occasionally overlap with it.
items.explanation: AL amyloidosis is a plasma cell disorder closely related to multiple myeloma.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.statement: In untreated patients, the median survival from onset of heart failure due to AL amyloidosis is approximately 2 years.
items.explanation: The median survival for untreated patients with cardiac involvement in AL amyloidosis is ~6 months.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.statement: Wild-type TTR amyloidosis primarily affects women.
items.explanation: Wild-type TTR amyloidosis mainly affects men, who are almost 20 times more likely to be diagnosed than women.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.statement: In wild-type TTR amyloidosis, the lungs and gut often show extensive amyloid deposits upon histologic examination.
items.explanation: Histologic examination of the lungs and gut in wild-type TTR amyloidosis often shows fairly extensive amyloid deposits.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.statement: Echocardiography can be sufficient for proceeding to further laboratory evaluation for amyloidosis.
items.explanation: The typical echocardiographic appearance may warrant further laboratory evaluation.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: In cardiac amyloidosis, the left ventricular ejection fraction is always significantly reduced.
items.explanation: The left ventricular ejection fraction may be normal or near-normal in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.statement: Renal involvement is a feature of transthyretin amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Renal involvement is not a feature of transthyretin amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.statement: In AL amyloidosis, the sedimentation rate is usually markedly elevated.
items.explanation: The sedimentation rate in AL amyloidosis is usually normal or only minimally elevated.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.statement: Sodium-glucose cotransporter 2 (SGLT2) inhibitors have been shown to be clinically well-tolerated in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.explanation: The use of SGLT2 inhibitors appears to be clinically well-tolerated and may be valuable in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.statement: Prophylactic implantable defibrillators are generally effective in preventing sudden cardiac death in patients with advanced AL amyloidosis.
items.explanation: A prophylactic implantable defibrillator is generally ineffective in preventing sudden cardiac death in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.statement: Dexamethasone is used in the treatment regimen for light chain amyloidosis and may aggravate sodium retention.
items.explanation: Dexamethasone can aggravate sodium retention, requiring careful management.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.statement: TTR silencers are approved for the treatment of transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy.
items.explanation: TTR silencers are approved in the U.S. only for familial amyloid polyneuropathy, not specifically for cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS
items.assertion: The atria are usually dilated in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reason: This is due to the chronic elevation of biventricular diastolic filling pressure and may further dilate with atrial arrhythmias.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the atria are usually dilated in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · AMYLOIDOSIS | PATHOLOGIC MECHANISMS OF HEART FAILURE IN CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS

items.subtopic: DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART
items.assertion: Periorbital purpura is virtually pathognomonic of AL amyloidosis.
items.reason: It occurs in 20–30% of cases and indicates the presence of a multisystem disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why periorbital purpura is considered pathognomonic for AL amyloidosis.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | AL AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.assertion: Autonomic neuropathy can reduce peripheral resistance, thereby decreasing the work of the heart.
items.reason: Myocardial infiltration may be extensive in patients with severe variant transthyretin neuropathy but cardiac symptoms can be relatively mild due to autonomic neuropathy reducing peripheral resistance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why cardiac symptoms might be mild despite extensive myocardial infiltration.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.assertion: Men with wild-type TTR amyloidosis have a more indolent course compared to women.
items.reason: When wild-type TTR amyloidosis occurs in women, it tends to present at a later age and has a more indolent course.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; men are more likely to have wild-type TTR amyloidosis with a less indolent course compared to when it occurs in women.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · TTR AMYLOIDOSIS OF THE HEART

items.assertion: Echocardiography shows increased right ventricle wall thickness in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reason: The right ventricle may show increased thickness of the free wall in cardiac amyloidosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Cardiac magnetic resonance is not used in diagnosing suspected cardiomyopathy.
items.reason: Cardiac magnetic resonance demonstrates marked expansion of the extracellular space due to amyloid deposition.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true as cardiac magnetic resonance is indeed used for this purpose.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A
items.assertion: A biopsy positive for AL amyloid from a noncardiac site is adequate to conclude cardiac involvement.
items.reason: Imaging consistent with amyloid cardiomyopathy supports the diagnosis of cardiac involvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · DIAGNOSIS AND TYPING OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS | A

items.assertion: Beta-blockade is often problematic and associated with decreased exercise tolerance in patients with cardiac amyloidosis.
items.reason: Patients are usually prescribed beta blockers when initially seen, but their use tends to decrease the patient's sense of well-being.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why beta-blockade is problematic.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.assertion: Patients with TTR amyloidosis should be considered for cardiac pacing if they experience unexplained dizzy spells or syncope.
items.reason: These symptoms, when occurring in the setting of bifascicular block or left bundle branch block on ECG, indicate a high risk for developing high-degree atrioventricular block.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why cardiac pacing should be considered.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · Cardiac Amyloidosis

items.subtopic: SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
items.assertion: Patients with light chain amyloidosis and cardiac involvement need a pretherapy cardiac evaluation.
items.reason: This evaluation assesses the risk-benefit ratio of high-dose melphalan chemotherapy with autologous stem cell transplantation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the evaluation is necessary.
items.reference: Amyloidosis and Other Restrictive Cardiomyopathies · SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: AL AMYLOIDOSIS | SPECIFIC THERAPY OF CARDIAC AMYLOIDOSIS: TTR AMYLOIDOSIS
